---
title: "DTSC 560 Module 4 Assignment: Time Series Analysis"
Student Name:
output: html_notebook
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# R Notebook Instructions

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

Try executing a chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or
by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*.

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or
by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output
will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press
*Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the
editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code
chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the
editor is displayed.

You can show or hide the outline using the "Outline" button inside of
the source pane.

You can view this notebook in Source mode or in Visual mode by clicking
on the view you want to use in the Source pane.

# DTSC560 Assignment Directions

Use this template to complete the assignment, following the directions provided in Brightspace. To unlock the quiz, you must first upload the assignment file. The upload itself is not graded; instead, you will answer quiz questions based on the output generated in this notebook. You may add additional text and code blocks as needed.

# Assignment Set Up

load libraries, set working directory

```{r}

library(forecast)
library(TTR)
```

# Part 1:

Read in data set

```{r}
airfare <- read.csv("airfare.csv")

head(airfare)

names(airfare)

str(airfare)

```

## Step 1)

```{r}
# 
plot(airfare$Year,
     airfare$Airfare,
     type = "o",
     col = "red",
     xlab = "Year",
     ylab = "Average Airfare ($)",
     main = "Average U.S. Domestic Airfare (2004–2018)")

plot()
```

### Quiz Question #1:
The series fluctuates over time with an overall downward trend and no seasonality.

## Step 2)

```{r}
# 
# airfare$SMA3 <- round(TTR::SMA(airfare$Airfare, n = 3), 2)
# 
# airfare
# 
# forecast_2019_SMA <- mean(tail(airfare$Airfare, 3))
# 
# paste0("$", formatC(forecast_2019_SMA,
#                     format = "f",
#                     digits = 2,
#                     big.mark = ","))

# 3-year Simple Moving Average (Forecasting)
airfare$SMA3 <- c(NA, NA, NA)

for(i in 4:nrow(airfare)){
  airfare$SMA3[i] <- mean(airfare$Airfare[(i-3):(i-1)])
}

# Forecast for 2019
forecast_2019_SMA <- mean(tail(airfare$Airfare, 3))

paste0("$", formatC(forecast_2019_SMA,
                    format = "f",
                    digits = 2,
                    big.mark = ","))
# 
```

### Quiz Question #2:
$353.76

## Step 3)

```{r}

# Forecast errors
airfare$Error <- airfare$Airfare - airfare$SMA3

# Keep only rows where forecasts exist
accuracy_data <- airfare[!is.na(airfare$SMA3), ]

# Accuracy measures
MAE <- mean(abs(accuracy_data$Error))
MSE <- mean(accuracy_data$Error^2)
RMSE <- sqrt(MSE)
MAPE <- mean(abs(accuracy_data$Error / accuracy_data$Airfare)) * 100

# Print results
cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE), "%\n")
# airfare$Error <- airfare$Airfare - airfare$SMA3
# 
# # Remove rows with NA values because not counting in 3 year simple moving average
# accuracy_data <- na.omit(airfare)
# 
# MAE <- mean(abs(accuracy_data$Error))
# MSE <- mean(accuracy_data$Error^2)
# RMSE <- sqrt(MSE)
# MAPE <- mean(abs(accuracy_data$Error / accuracy_data$Airfare)) * 100
# 
# # Printing results
# cat("MAE :", round(MAE, 2), "\n")
# cat("MSE :", round(MSE, 2), "\n")
# cat("RMSE:", round(RMSE, 2), "\n")
# cat("MAPE:", round(MAPE, 2), "%\n")

```

### Quiz Question #3:
MAE is 10.41

## Step 4)

```{r}
# Simple Exponential Smoothing with alpha = 0.2
ses_model_02 <- HoltWinters(airfare$Airfare,
                            alpha = 0.2,
                            beta = FALSE,
                            gamma = FALSE)

forecast_2019_SES02 <- predict(ses_model_02, n.ahead = 1)

forecast_2019_SES02

# Used for formatting
paste0("$",
       formatC(forecast_2019_SES02,
               format = "f",
               digits = 2,
               big.mark = ","))


```

### Quiz Question #4:
$376.64 is 2019 average forecast

## Step 5)

```{r}
# Fitted values
fitted_02 <- fitted(ses_model_02)[,1]

# Actual values (exclude first observation)
actual_02 <- airfare$Airfare[-1]

# Errors
errors_02 <- actual_02 - fitted_02

# Accuracy measures
MAE_SES02  <- mean(abs(errors_02))
MSE_SES02  <- mean(errors_02^2)
RMSE_SES02 <- sqrt(MSE_SES02)
MAPE_SES02 <- mean(abs(errors_02 / actual_02)) * 100

cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE_SES02), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE_SES02), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE_SES02), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE_SES02), "%\n")
```

### Quiz Question #5:
MAE is 18.25

## Step 6)

```{r}
# Simple Exponential Smoothing with alpha = 0.8
ses_model_08 <- HoltWinters(
  airfare$Airfare,
  alpha = 0.8,
  beta = FALSE,
  gamma = FALSE
)

# Forecast one year ahead (2019)
forecast_2019_SES08 <- predict(ses_model_08, n.ahead = 1)

forecast_2019_SES08

# Used for formatting
paste0("$",
       formatC(forecast_2019_SES08,
               format = "f",
               digits = 2,
               big.mark = ","))

```

### Quiz Question #6:
$348.42

## Step 7)

```{r}
# Fitted values for alpha = 0.8
fitted_08 <- fitted(ses_model_08)[,1]

# Actual values (exclude first observation)
actual_08 <- airfare$Airfare[-1]

# Errors
errors_08 <- actual_08 - fitted_08

# Accuracy measures
MAE_SES08  <- mean(abs(errors_08))
MSE_SES08  <- mean(errors_08^2)
RMSE_SES08 <- sqrt(MSE_SES08)
MAPE_SES08 <- mean(abs(errors_08 / actual_08)) * 100

# Print results
cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE_SES08), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE_SES08), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE_SES08), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE_SES08), "%\n")

```

### Quiz Question #7:

MAE = 15.6

### Quiz Question #8:
The forecasting method with the lowest error measures (especially MAE/RMSE/MAPE) should be trusted most.

# Part 2

Read in data set

```{r}
warner <- read.csv("warner_music.csv")

head(warner)

names(warner)

str(warner)

```

## Step 8)

```{r}
plot(warner$Revenue,
     type = "o",              
     col = "steelblue",            
     pch = 16,                
     lwd = 2,                 
     xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Quarter",
     ylab = "Revenue (Million USD)",
     main = "Warner Music Group Quarterly Revenue")

axis(1,
     at = 1:nrow(warner),
     labels = warner$Quarter,
     las = 2,
     cex.axis = 0.7)

grid(col = "gray85", lty = "dotted")
```

### Quiz Question #9:
There is an upward trend and a seasonal pattern.

## Step 9)

```{r}
warner$Trend <- 1:nrow(warner)

trend_model <- lm(Revenue ~ Trend, data = warner)

summary(trend_model)

round(coef(trend_model), 2)
```

### Quiz Question #10: 
The slope coefficient for the trend variable is 23.19

## Step 10)

```{r}
warner$Predicted_Trend <- predict(trend_model)

warner$Error <- warner$Revenue - warner$Predicted_Trend

MAE <- mean(abs(warner$Error))
MSE <- mean(warner$Error^2)
RMSE <- sqrt(MSE)
MAPE <- mean(abs(warner$Error / warner$Revenue)) * 100

cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE), "%\n")
```

### Quiz Question #11:
The RMSE is 72.07
## Step 11)

```{r}
warner$Season <- substr(warner$Quarter, 5, 6)

table(warner$Season)

aggregate(Revenue ~ Season, data = warner, mean)

# Set the reference quarter and setting Q4 as it has the highest revenue
warner$Season <- relevel(factor(warner$Season), ref = "Q4")

season_model <- lm(Revenue ~ Trend + Season, data = warner)

summary(season_model)

round(coef(season_model), 2)

```

### Quiz Question #12:
Holding the trend constant, the average revenue in Quarter 2 is estimated to be 34.05 million USD lower than the average revenue in Quarter 4 (the reference quarter).

## Step 12)

```{r}
warner$Predicted_Season <- predict(season_model)

warner$Error_Season <- warner$Revenue - warner$Predicted_Season

MAE_Season <- mean(abs(warner$Error_Season))
MSE_Season <- mean(warner$Error_Season^2)
RMSE_Season <- sqrt(MSE_Season)
MAPE_Season <- mean(abs(warner$Error_Season / warner$Revenue)) * 100

cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE_Season), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE_Season), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE_Season), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE_Season), "%\n")

```

### Quiz Question #13:
RMSE value is 48.41

### Quiz Question #14:
The regression model including both trend and seasonality provides a better fit and more accurate forecasts than the model using trend only as we can see the rmse from step 12 is quite lower than the rmse calculated in step 10.

## Step 13)

```{r}
future <- data.frame(
  Trend = (nrow(warner)+1):(nrow(warner)+4),
  Season = factor(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),
                  levels = levels(warner$Season))
)

future$Forecast <- predict(season_model, newdata = future)

# Round to two decimal places
future$Forecast <- round(future$Forecast, 2)

future

paste0("$", future$Forecast[4]) # Forecast revenue value for Q4 2022


```

### Quiz Question #15:
Forecast revenue for Q4 2022 is $1405.56

# Part 3

Read in data set

```{r}
aws <- read.csv("amazon_web_services.csv")

head(aws)

names(aws)

str(aws)

```

## Step 14)

```{r}
plot(aws$Revenue,
     type = "o",
     col = "firebrick",
     pch = 16,
     lwd = 2,
     xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Quarter",
     ylab = "Revenue (Million USD)",
     main = "Amazon Web Services Quarterly Revenue")

axis(1,
     at = 1:nrow(aws),
     labels = aws$Quarter,
     las = 2,
     cex.axis = 0.7)

grid(col = "gray85", lty = "dotted")

```

## Step 15)

```{r}
aws$Trend <- 1:nrow(aws)

trend_model_aws <- lm(Revenue ~ Trend, data = aws)

summary(trend_model_aws)

round(coef(trend_model_aws), 2)

```

### Quiz Question #16:
497.89 is the slope coefficient for the trend variable

## Step 16)

```{r}

aws$Predicted_Trend <- predict(trend_model_aws)

aws$Error <- aws$Revenue - aws$Predicted_Trend

MAE <- mean(abs(aws$Error))
MSE <- mean(aws$Error^2)
RMSE <- sqrt(MSE)
MAPE <- mean(abs(aws$Error / aws$Revenue)) * 100

cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE), "%\n")

```

### Quiz Question #17:
The MAPE value is 31.39%.

## Step 17)

```{r}
aws$Trend2 <- aws$Trend^2

# Quadratic regression
quad_model <- lm(Revenue ~ Trend + Trend2, data = aws)

summary(quad_model)

round(coef(quad_model), 2)

plot(aws$Trend,
     aws$Revenue,
     pch = 16,
     col = "firebrick",
     xlab = "Trend",
     ylab = "Revenue (Million USD)",
     main = "Quadratic Trend - Amazon Web Services")

lines(aws$Trend,
      predict(quad_model),
      col = "red",
      lwd = 2)

grid(col = "gray85", lty = "dotted")

```

### Quiz Question #18: 
As you can see from the plot that the revenue is increasing over time it is an accelerating growth so a quadratic regression is the most appropriate model for this analysis.

## Step 18)

```{r}
aws$Predicted_Quad <- predict(quad_model)

aws$Error_Quad <- aws$Revenue - aws$Predicted_Quad

MAE_Quad <- mean(abs(aws$Error_Quad))
MSE_Quad <- mean(aws$Error_Quad^2)
RMSE_Quad <- sqrt(MSE_Quad)
MAPE_Quad <- mean(abs(aws$Error_Quad / aws$Revenue)) * 100

cat("MAE :", sprintf("%.2f", MAE_Quad), "\n")
cat("MSE :", sprintf("%.2f", MSE_Quad), "\n")
cat("RMSE:", sprintf("%.2f", RMSE_Quad), "\n")
cat("MAPE:", sprintf("%.2f", MAPE_Quad), "%\n")

```

### Quiz Question #19:
The MAPE value based on the analysis calculation is 5.21%.

## Step 19)

```{r}
future_aws <- data.frame(
  Trend = (nrow(warner)+1):(nrow(warner)+4)
)

future_aws$Trend2 <- future_aws$Trend^2

future_aws$Forecast <- predict(quad_model, newdata = future_aws)

future_aws$Forecast <- round(future_aws$Forecast, 2)

future_aws

paste0("$", future_aws$Forecast[3])

```

### Quiz Question #20:
Forecast revenue value for Q3 2022 is $13034.03

This material is for enrolled students' academic use only and protected
under U.S. Copyright Laws. This content must not be shared outside the
confines of this course, in line with Eastern University's academic
integrity policies. Unauthorized reproduction, distribution, or
transmission of this material, including but not limited to posting on
third-party platforms like GitHub, is strictly prohibited and may lead
to disciplinary action. You may not alter or remove any copyright or
other notice from copies of any content taken from BrightSpace or
Eastern University’s website. © Copyright Notice 2024, Eastern
University - All Rights Reserved
